Advisor: Great. And Susan, anything on your side you want to make sure we cover?

Advisor: Hi Robert, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d say: confirm the big numbers, make sure we’re not missing anything obvious, and leave with clear next steps.
Client 2: Yeah—same. And just… keep it practical. I don’t want it to turn into a super technical thing right away.

Advisor: Great. Before we lock that in, I want to grab a few household basics I didn’t want to assume, Robert and Susan.
Advisor: First—marital status for planning: are you two married, or partnered another way?
Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And how many dependents are we planning for right now—kids or anyone else financially dependent on you?

Client 1: Conservative. I don’t want big drawdowns.
Advisor: Understood. Let’s shift into the numbers so I can build the scenarios cleanly.
Advisor: For income, I have Robert at $205,500 gross annually, Susan at $112,400 gross annually self-employed, plus a monthly bonus that totals $86,850 annually, and Robert’s interest and dividends at $231,050 annually.
Client 1: Yes, those are the numbers—$205,500 for me, $112,400 for Susan, $86,850 annual bonus paid monthly, and $231,050 annual interest and dividends paid monthly.
Advisor: Great—thank you. On the asset side, Robert, I’m showing four accounts in your name: $517,250 in a taxable account on a retirement platform, $377,050 in a 401(k)/IRA bucket also on a retirement platform, $93,700 at an insurance company in a bank-type account, and $167,350 in private markets held at a bank. And then the house jointly at $951,750.
